messages.content:
Hi everyone,

Since now the stage1 has finished, I figure it is a good time for us to openly share the interesting results/findings we got from stage1 experiments.

I'll start by sharing a interesting candidate cheatsheet that I obtained during an evolutionary search:

https://competition.sair.foundation/contributor-network/mathematics-distillation-challenge-equational-theories-stage1/EQT01-000145

It might not be the strongest candidate by the stage1 rule (evenly weighted average score across models and datasets), but it seems to have incredible generalizability cross the LLM models, whenever I tested it,

Here is its performances on stage 1 public sets, plus SairCommunity Bench created by @Adam Betka

Model normal hard1 hard2 hard3 SairCommunityBench
gpt-oss-120b 80.30% (803/1000) 44.93% (31/69) 70.00% (140/200) 57.00% (228/400) 77.50% (155/200)
llama3.3-70b-instruct 76.00% (760/1000) 42.03% (29/69) 66.50% (133/200) 57.00% (228/400) 75.00% (150/200)
gemma4-31b-it 79.80% (798/1000) 42.03% (29/69) 69.00% (138/200) 57.25% (229/400) 75.50% (151/200)

As you could see, every accuracy number is very similar on each test set , even though the llama3.3 would be a little bit dumber in general.

It would be very helpful if the community could help me check its performance on some other LLMs (e.g, DeepSeek-V3.2) as well. (Because I've burned enough tokens for this lmao)

If this candidate is tested to actually have good generalization ability across LLM models, It's also interesting if @YZ could help investigate from mathematical point of view why this is the happening; and also for the Machine Learning Engineers to investigate why, from LLM/Representation point of views.

Cheers!
messages.subject: Interesting stage1 findings

| gemma4-31b-it          | 79.80% (798/1000) | 42.03% (29/69) | 69.00% (138/200) | 57.25% (229/400) | 75.50% (151/200)   |

As you could see, every accuracy number is very similar on each test set , even though the llama3.3 would be a little bit dumber in general.

It would be very helpful if the community could help me check its performance on some other LLMs (e.g, DeepSeek-V3.2) as well. (Because I've burned enough tokens for this lmao)

If this candidate is tested to actually have good generalization ability across LLM models,  It's also interesting if @**YZ** could help investigate from mathematical point of view why this is the happening.

messages.content: Gan W. has marked this topic as unresolved.
messages.subject: Interesting stage1 findings

messages.content:
@YZ there are some wild results comparing the final cheat sheets submitted using the playground tools. It’s probably too late for stage 1, but having a leaderboard for identical playground results would be interesting :thinking:. Using the playground only during stage 1, the contribution leaderboard and final leaderboard are dramatically different from the playground results. This could be the same for stage 2 most likely. Playground history leaves more comparable data than I could retrieve comparing the final results leaderboard available data, though the parameters are completely different.

Like, I’m pretty confident my submitted sheet is a top‑tier fastest resolver and the only sheet with a potential security key sector included, though it’s difficult to compare solving speeds if you’re not solving at the same maximum capacity as the other sheets, meh I also hit a logic‑gate issue with hard3 sets where final verdict would require a forced‑verdict swapping haha and I traded .kb sheet size for quicker solving speed, I covered fewer edge cases for formatting, stage2 in pythons a great change, overall stage1 very interesting had success solving with a new ternary logic system, wp yoos.
messages.subject: Interesting stage1 findings

This could be the same for stage2 most likely, playground history leaves more comparable data than I could retrieve comparing th final results leaderboard though the parameters are completely different. Like I'm pretty confident my submitted sheet is top tier fastest resolver and the only sheet with a potential security key sector included, though it's difficult to compare solving speeds if you're not solving at the same maximum capacity of the other sheets, also I hit a logic gate issue with hard3 sets where final verdict would require a force verdict swapping haha  and traded .kb sheet size for quicker solving speed i covered less edge cases for formating, also had success solving with a new ternary logic system, thanks for stage1 wp yoos

messages.content:
@Stacey Szmy Thanks for the suggestion, and for sharing these observations.

The Stage 2 Playground is now open, and we’ll share a tutorial soon. If you run into any issues while using it, please let us know. We’re working to improve the platform and tools so participants can have a better user experience.
messages.subject: Interesting stage1 findings
